food_allergy.other_please_list: Allergic to lupin (legume related to peanuts). Intolerance to large amounts of artificial food dyes (especially Red 40) – causes headaches and stomach upset but not anaphylaxis.
food_allergy.other_special_diet_needs_or_restrictions: Participant avoids all peanuts and tree nuts (including but not limited to almonds, cashews, pistachios, walnuts, pecans, hazelnuts, macadamia nuts, Brazil nuts, pine nuts) and products processed in facilities that also process peanuts or tree nuts whenever possible. Also avoids lupin flour and lupin-containing baked goods due to cross-reactive legume allergy confirmed by allergist. Artificial food dyes are limited; brightly colored candies and drinks (e.g., red sports drinks, neon-colored gummies) are avoided because they trigger migraines and mild gastrointestinal upset. No religious or philosophical dietary restrictions. Participant eats a varied diet including dairy, eggs, wheat, soy, fruits, vegetables, and meats as long as they are nut- and peanut-free.

dietary_needs_questionnaire.what_are_the_preferred_food_substitution:
• Nut butters: Use sunflower seed butter or roasted chickpea spread instead of peanut butter or almond butter. At home we primarily use SunButter (sunflower seed spread). At school and camp, any seed-based spread that is clearly labeled “nut-free” is acceptable.

• Snack bars and granola: Use nut-free granola bars (e.g., brands that are specifically labeled peanut- and tree-nut-free) or simple snacks like pretzels, popcorn, or fruit instead of mixed-nut granola bars or trail mix.

• Pesto and sauces: Use basil pesto made without pine nuts (or any nuts) or substitute with a simple olive oil, garlic, and herb sauce instead of traditional nut-containing pesto.

• Baked goods: Provide muffins, cookies, and breads made in a nut-free facility instead of those that contain nuts or may contain traces. Oatmeal raisin, chocolate chip (without nuts), banana bread, and plain bagels are all good options.

• Protein snacks: Cheese sticks, yogurt, hummus with veggies, hard-boiled eggs, and turkey roll-ups are preferred protein options instead of nut mixes or peanut-containing protein bars.

• Treats/candy: Plain chocolate that is made in a nut-free facility, fruit snacks, or simple cookies are preferred instead of candy bars that contain peanuts or tree nuts (such as Snickers, Reese’s, M&M’s with peanuts or almonds, etc.).

• For dyes: For drinks and desserts, use clear or lightly colored beverages (like lemonade or apple juice) and naturally colored treats (like fruit popsicles) instead of bright red or neon-colored sports drinks, slushies, or candies with heavy artificial dyes.
dietary_needs_questionnaire.please_explain:
The participant has a confirmed IgE-mediated allergy to peanuts and multiple tree nuts, as well as a clinically significant reaction to lupin. Reactions have occurred only with ingestion (eating or drinking) of the allergen, but we are careful about cross-contact because a very small amount of peanut or tree nut in food has caused symptoms in the past.

• Airborne exposure: Simply being in the same room as peanuts or tree nuts (for example, someone else eating a peanut butter sandwich nearby) has not caused a reaction so far. However, we avoid situations where there is heavy airborne peanut dust, such as large bowls of shelled peanuts being cracked open, because our allergist has advised that this could be higher risk. In typical school or camp settings, regular proximity without direct contact has not triggered symptoms.

• Trace cross-contact: Trace amounts of peanut or tree nut residue in food have caused mild reactions. For example, when he was 8, he ate a cookie that was labeled “may contain peanuts” and developed hives around his mouth and mild stomach cramps within about 20 minutes. Another time, he had a slice of cake at a birthday party that had been cut with the same knife used on a peanut butter cake; he developed itchy hives on his face and neck. Because of this, we avoid foods with advisory labels such as “may contain peanuts/tree nuts,” “processed on shared equipment,” or “made in a facility that also processes peanuts or tree nuts” when there is a safer alternative.

• Actual ingestion: A clear anaphylactic reaction occurred at age 6 after accidentally eating a small bite of a peanut-containing candy bar. Symptoms included lip swelling, hives on the torso, coughing, and difficulty breathing. He was treated in the emergency room and now always carries two epinephrine auto-injectors. Another moderate reaction (hives, vomiting, wheezing) occurred at age 10 after eating ice cream that was later found to be cross-contaminated with a nut-based flavor. These events make it very important to prevent any ingestion of peanuts, tree nuts, or lupin.

In day-to-day life, we manage the allergy by reading all ingredient labels carefully, checking for advisory statements about peanuts and tree nuts, and choosing brands that are known to be nut-free or produced in dedicated nut-free facilities. At camp or school, we rely on clear communication with the kitchen and staff, careful preparation with clean utensils, and avoiding obvious nut-containing foods like peanut butter sandwiches, granola with nuts, nut-based desserts, and mixed trail mixes.
dietary_needs_questionnaire.does_the_participant_understand_the_food:
Yes. The participant, 13-year-old Ethan Miller, has lived with this allergy since early childhood and has a strong understanding of what he can and cannot safely eat. He recognizes that peanuts, tree nuts, and lupin are dangerous for him and can explain in his own words that they can cause trouble breathing and might send him to the hospital.

Ethan:
• Always asks about ingredients before eating something new, especially baked goods, desserts, and sauces.
• Knows to avoid foods if the label says “may contain peanuts or tree nuts” or “processed in a facility with peanuts or tree nuts,” unless a parent or trusted adult has specifically checked and approved it.
• Understands the importance of not sharing food, drinks, or utensils with friends.
• Wears a medical alert bracelet that lists “Peanut and Tree Nut Allergy – Carries EpiPen.”

In terms of emergency management, Ethan:
• Knows where his epinephrine auto-injectors are at all times (he carries one set on his person in a small waist pack; a backup set is usually stored with the nurse or designated adult).
• Has practiced with a trainer device and can demonstrate how to use his epinephrine auto-injector on his own thigh, though he prefers that an adult administer it if possible.
• Can list his early symptoms: tingling or itching in the mouth, tightness in the throat, hives, stomach ache, and feeling suddenly anxious or “off.”
• Understands that if he thinks he might be having a reaction, he must tell an adult immediately (counselor, teacher, coach, or nurse) and not wait to see if it gets better on its own.

At home, he helps read labels and is involved in planning safe meals and snacks, so he is very used to checking for allergens. While he is responsible for speaking up and being cautious, we still expect adults in charge (camp staff, kitchen staff, teachers) to take the lead on reading labels, preventing cross-contact, and administering medication in an emergency.
dietary_needs_questionnaire.if_yes_how_were_the_meals_handled:
Ethan has attended day camp for the past three summers and regularly eats school lunches during the school year, so he is used to eating meals prepared outside the home.

At school:
• His parents met with the school nurse and cafeteria manager at the beginning of each year to review his allergy action plan and discuss safe options.
• The cafeteria provides a list of menu items and ingredient labels, and the staff is trained to check for peanuts and tree nuts before serving him.
• Ethan typically chooses simple, lower-risk items such as plain hamburgers or grilled chicken (no sauces that might contain nuts), cheese pizza, plain pasta with marinara, fresh fruit, yogurt, and salads without nuts.
• He sits at a table where nut-containing foods are discouraged, and his friends know not to bring peanut butter sandwiches or nut-heavy snacks when eating with him.

At previous day camps:
• Before camp started, his parents completed a detailed allergy form and spoke directly with the camp director and camp nurse.
• The camp kitchen was informed that Ethan cannot have any peanuts, tree nuts, or nut-based products. They were asked to:
  – Avoid serving peanut butter as a main option when possible.
  – Clearly label any foods that contain nuts.
  – Use clean utensils, cutting boards, and prep surfaces before preparing his meals.
• Ethan brought his own nut-free snacks for situations where the group snack included granola bars, trail mix, or cookies that might contain nuts.
• Two epinephrine auto-injectors were stored with the nurse, and Ethan also carried a set in a waist pack when allowed; counselors were trained on recognizing symptoms and how to respond.

At restaurants and social events:
• The family usually chooses restaurants with clear allergy policies and simpler menus (for example, places that can easily make a plain grilled chicken, burger, or pasta dish without risk of nuts).
• They always inform the server about his peanut and tree nut allergy as soon as they sit down and ask that the kitchen avoid any cross-contact (clean pan, separate utensils).
• Ethan avoids desserts that are commonly cross-contaminated, such as bakery-case cakes, pastries, and ice cream parlors that scoop multiple nut flavors from shared containers.

For this camp/program, we would like meals handled similarly: clear labeling of any nut-containing foods, avoidance of peanut butter as a main staple when possible, careful attention to cross-contact in the kitchen, and a designated adult who knows Ethan’s allergy plan, where his epinephrine auto-injectors are stored, and how to use them in an emergency.
